channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: EA boasts record year thanks to Battlefield 6 and EA Sports FC
item.content:encoded:
EA has published its preliminary financial results for the fiscal year ended March 31, 2026.

According to a press release, net bookings totalled a record-breaking $8 billion in FY26, up 9 percent year-over-year. Net revenue for FY26 was $7.5 billion, up 1 percent year over year. Battlefield 6's impressive launch and performance were lauded, as were the performances of EA Sports FC 26, FC Online, and FC Mobile. 

Apex Legends earned a mention as well after delivering its "strongest net bookings quarter of the fiscal year in Q4."

"Driven by our talented teams and disciplined execution, we delivered a record FY26, highlighted by the incredibly successful launch of our iconic Battlefield franchise," said EA CEO Andrew Wilson.

"With the recent completion of a debt process that was met with strong investor demand and our ongoing constructive engagement with regulators, we look ahead to closing the transaction and the opportunities it will unlock."

Related:EVE Online studio CCP Games turns independent and rebrands as Fenris Creations

That success comes off the back of job cuts. Earlier this year, EA laid off an undisclosed number of Battlefield developers just months after the record-breaking launch of Battlefield 6.

The company will not be hosting a conference call this quarter due to its pending acquisition by an investor consortium that includes Saudi Arabian sovereign wealth fund, PIF.

Numerous lawmakers and leaders have expressed concern about the take-private deal, including labor union CWA Canada and numerous US congress members.

About the Author

Editor-in-Chief, GameDeveloper.com

Danielle is the editor-in-chief of Game Developer, with previous editorial posts at Fanbyte, VICE, and Polygon. She’s also a lecturer in game design at the Berklee College of Music, and a hobbyist game developer in her spare time.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/ea-boasts-record-year-thanks-to-battlefield-6-and-ea-sports-fc
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: EVE Online studio CCP Games turns independent and rebrands as Fenris Creations
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

May 6, 2026

3 Min Read

Image via Fenris Creations

EVE Online developer CCP Games is becoming an independent studio and rebranding as Fenris Creations.

The Icelandic company was sold back to its management team by former owner Pearl Abyss in a deal worth $120 million. The South Korean publisher acquired CCP Games in 2018 for around $425 million. 

Now, after regaining its independence, Fenris will operate as a standalone studio responsible for its own strategy, operations, and creative direction. 

"Fenris Creations is now governed by its own Board of Directors, returning to a model similar to how the company operated before 2018, and one designed to support strategic decision-making for persistent live games and long-running virtual worlds," reads an announcement on the Fenris website.

"The ownership group comprises Fenris Creations’ senior management and long-term investors aligned with the company's future as a developer, publisher, and operator of player-driven online experiences."

Related:CWA Canada president says Saudi buyout of EA should trigger 'virtually every alarm bell'

Fenris CEO Hilmar Veigar Pétursson said the studio will not be making layoffs or restructuring as a result of the transition and said EVE Online closed 2025 with some of its best financial results in years after delivering record revenue in November. The company ended the year with over $70 million in reported revenue.

"There are no planned changes to the company’s organizational structure, and its headquarters will remain in Vatnsmýrin, Iceland. Studios in Reykjavík, London, and Shanghai will continue to operate as they do today." the announcement adds. 

Fenris will continue to support the EVE franchise 'with forever in mind'

Notably, Fenris has also entered into a research partnership with Google DeepMind that will see Google acquire a minority stake in the company. The financial terms of the partnership were not disclosed, but Fenris said the duo will focus on "advancing understanding of intelligence in complex, dynamic systems."

"The collaboration will explore areas including long-horizon planning, memory, and continual learning, using EVE Online as a uniquely rich environment for study. Google DeepMind will work with an offline version of EVE Online running on a local server to test and evaluate models in a controlled setting," the announcement continues. 

"Together, the partnership will also explore new gameplay experiences enabled by these technologies."

Deepmind is the artificial intelligence division of Google parent company Alphabet Inc. It has developed a number of generative AI tools such as image generator Nano Banana 2 and world model Genie 3, which Google claims is capable of creating digital worlds that can be explored at will.

Related:Xbox appoints new internal leaders and ditches Copilot AI

 Looking ahead, Pétursson said the EVE franchise is "built to endure" and will continue to be supported over the coming decades. 

"This transition gives us direct ownership, clear accountability, and the independence to invest in worlds that grow over decades,” said Pétursson.

"We're grateful to Pearl Abyss for their partnership and for the consistent support they’ve shown us over the past seven and a half years. EVE Online exists today because of pioneering thinking, patience, and trust between developers and players. Our new structure and partners enable us to carry that legacy forward—continuously evolving a living universe and actively exploring what it can become, with forever in mind."

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/eve-online-studio-ccp-games-turns-independent-and-rebrands-as-fenris-creations
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: CWA Canada president says Saudi buyout of EA should trigger 'virtually every alarm bell'
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

May 6, 2026

4 Min Read

Labor union CWA Canada has called on the Canadian government to scrutinize the proposed $55 billion buyout of Electronic Arts over perceived national security risks. 

The Battlefield and EA Sports FC developer is being acquired by a consortium that includes Saudi Arabia's state-owned investment vehicle, Public Investment Fund (PIF), and private investment firms Silver Lake and Affinity Partners—the latter of which was established by Jared Kushner, the son-in-law of U.S. president Donald Trump. 

EA currently operates multiple studios across Canada in locations such as Quebec, Vancouver, Toronto, and Victoria.

In a letter sent to Canadian minister of industry Mélanie Joly on May 5, 2026, CWA Canada president Carmel Smyth said the union has "serious concerns" about the national security implications of the proposed takeover. 

"We are deeply concerned that a foreign sovereign wealth fund tasked with implementing the Saudi Arabian government's strategy to 'become the global hub for gaming and esports' plans to acquire one of the most successful North American video game publishers, with extensive operations in Canada, reaching hundreds of millions of players globally," reads the letter, shared with Game Developer via email. 

Related:EVE Online studio CCP Games turns independent and rebrands as Fenris Creations

"[...] In addition to the sheer size and strategy behind this deal, it is also highly relevant that, because the deal would put EA under the near-absolute control of PIF, not only would EA be closely economically integrated with the Saudi Arabian government, but control would effectively be solely in the hands of Crown Prince Mohammed Bin Salman, the unelected monarch of a country with, at best, a very different approach from Canada to personal autonomy and privacy and at worst a highly troubling record of human rights abuses including surveillance and misuse of information."

Advocacy groups such as Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch have strongly criticized Saudi Arabia's human rights record under the leadership of bin Salman, who they claim has suppressed freedom of speech by outlawing protests, criminalizing LGBTQ+ relationships and gender expression, and discriminating against women.

Bin Salman has also been linked to the 2018 murder of Washington Post journalist Jamal Khashoggi, and faced questions about his alleged involvement in the killing during a White House press conference in November 2025.

"The structure of the proposed acquisition puts the thousands of Canadian EA workers at increased risk"

In the letter, Smyth suggested the Saudi Arabian government "under the singular authority of Crown Prince Salman" is unlikely to operate EA in a way that is consistent with Canadian rights and values, and said the deal will allow the Saudi regime to collect and leverage the sensitive personal data of millions of Canadians.

Related:Xbox appoints new internal leaders and ditches Copilot AI

According to Smyth, that alone should be ringing "virtually every alarm bell regarding potential national security risks."

"Saudi Arabia could utilize its access to Canadians' personal information for purposes of mass surveillance, censorship, or propaganda, and we are highly skeptical Saudi Arabia would engage in these activities in a manner that is consistent with Canadian values," she continued. 

"Finally, because EA is heavily invested in developing artificial intelligence (AI), the transaction could also result in the transfer of sensitive technology to a foreign state, also an acute national security concern under the ICA." 

The 'ICA' refers to the Investment Canada Act, a Canadian federal law Smyth explained is designed to balance a positive investment climate while safeguarding Canada from "foreign actors that attempt to gain ownership or control of sensitive Canadian goods, technology, infrastructure, or personal data" for nefarious reasons.

Smyth claimed the EA buyout fits that profile—but she has other concerns over how the deal might impact the livelihoods of workers at the global publisher. 

Related:Valve to restock Steam Controller after it sells out in a day

"Workers in the video game industry have experienced significant layoffs in recent years, and the structure of the proposed acquisition puts the thousands of Canadian EA workers at increased risk," she continued. "The deal is being financed with US$20 billion in debt; as a result, some analysts suggest layoffs are one of the key ways the company will seek to cut costs in order to service its massive debt."

EA previously suggested the buyout won't result in "immediate" job cuts. It has since cut a number of roles across Battlefield Studios. The company also pledged to foster a culture of 'creativity, pioneering, passion, determination, learning, and teamwork' under its new owners. 

Even so, Smyth fears the Saudi Arabian state—which will own 93.4 percent of EA if the deal is approved—might begin using EA titles as vehicles for "mass surveillance and state-sponsored propaganda." As a result, she has urged the Canadian government to "blow the whistle" on the deal. 

This isn't the first time the buyout has been called into question. Last year, a group of unionized EA employees argued the proposed acquisition will likely harm workers and called on regulators in the United States to place it under the microscope. 

U.S. senators Richard Blumenthal and Elizabeth Warren also expressed concerns about the national security risks posed by the take-private. In January, another group of U.S. lawmakers said the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) must review the acquisition over fears it could incentive layoffs, offshoring, studio closures, and other cost-cutting measures.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/union-president-says-saudi-buyout-of-ea-should-trigger-virtually-every-alarm-bell-
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
